segments.title: A guide to Rome's eastern expansion
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces Gareth C. Sampson's study of the Roman eastern war of 74–62 BC and explains why its maps and accessible historical references make a dense subject approachable.
segments.body: The review presents the book as an inviting entry point for readers who know little Roman history as well as for those who recognise its allusions. Dr. Clarke highlights its maps and explanatory style before turning to the strategic scale of the conflict. The result is a guide to a war whose consequences reached well beyond a single Roman campaign.

evidence.note: He introduces the book, its subject, maps, and accessible explanations.

segments.title: Why this was more than a Roman-Pontic war
segments.summary: The book's opening frames the conflict as a region-wide eastern war, not simply the final Roman struggle with Pontus.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows the book's argument that the war expanded Rome from limited Asian footholds into a power stretching toward the Caucasus, Euphrates, and Red Sea. The conflict drew in Armenia, the Seleucid realm, Parthia, Judea, and Caucasian and Arabian peoples. That broader framing helps explain why the traditional label for the Third Mithridatic War can hide the war's strategic reach and its reshaping of the Hellenistic east.

evidence.note: The quoted introduction traces the conflict's expansion and explains the title Rome's Great Eastern War.

segments.title: Looking beyond Mithridates to Tigranes
segments.summary: A historiographical warning: a focus on Mithridates can obscure Tigranes II and the many political forces that sustained the eastern war.
segments.body: The review welcomes the book's resistance to a hero-and-villain account centred solely on Mithridates VI. Dr. Clarke notes that Tigranes II of Armenia was a more powerful ruler, yet receives less attention because his accommodation after defeat makes a less dramatic story. The book instead follows several leaders and the pressures that brought their states into a conflict lasting more than a decade.

evidence.note: He discusses the distortion created by a Mithridates-centred narrative and the importance of Tigranes and other actors.

segments.title: Interests, presence, and political decisions
segments.summary: The review uses Rome's eastern war to consider how states value allies, signal commitment, and make decisions through more than one visible leader.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that an interest another state appears ready to abandon may be essential to the state that holds it. He connects that uncertainty to deterrence and to the importance of showing up in the rooms where decisions are made. The discussion also cautions against treating presidents or prime ministers as the sole authors of policy when ministers and advisers shape the choices around them.

evidence.note: He reflects on empires, interests, deterrence, political presence, and collective decision-making.

segments.title: Understand the enemy and yourself
segments.summary: The central strategic lesson is that tactical success can fail when an opponent's political culture and willingness to continue fighting are misunderstood.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the book's eastern opponents with Japan in the Second World War: winning a major battle does not guarantee that the other state will concede. He describes Mithridates and Tigranes as gaining important tactical victories without understanding Rome's capacity and resolve to raise another army. The watch point connects ancient campaigning to a wider lesson about judging an enemy by its own strategic culture rather than by assumptions.

evidence.note: He draws the lesson to understand an opponent's society, strategic expectations, and capacity to continue the war.
